planting.note: Plant tuberous root divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; southern stoneroot is a tuberous-rooted mint-family herb of the southeastern coastal plain closely related to the better-known C. canadensis.

directions: Plant tuberous root divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade in zones 7–10; southern stoneroot is a mint-family perennial of the Gulf and South Atlantic coastal plain — found naturally in moist, shaded bottomlands and rich upland forests of Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, and adjacent states — that produces large, coarsely toothed, opposite leaves with a distinctive lemon-horse-balm fragrance and branching panicles of small, pale yellow flowers in late summer and fall. The large, knotty, tuberous root system gives the plant its common name and was used in traditional herbal medicine for digestive complaints, as with the related C. canadensis. It tolerates the heat and humidity of zone 8–10 better than its northern relative and is a useful large-leafed foliage plant for shaded southern gardens where few fragrant herbs thrive. Pair with native gingers, beautyberry, and spicebush for a fragrant native shade border.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; Appalachian bugbane has a very restricted natural range in shaded gorges and moist ravines in the central Appalachians (TN, NC, KY, AL) and is a rare but stunning native for large shaded gardens.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, deep, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Appalachian bugbane (formerly Cimicifuga rubifolia) has a very restricted natural range in shaded gorges, moist ravines, and rich mesic slopes in Tennessee, North Carolina, Kentucky, and Alabama, making it one of the rarest and most conservation-significant native garden plants. It is closely related to the widely grown Actaea racemosa (black cohosh) and produces similar dramatic, arching, bottle-brush spikes of tiny white flowers on stems 4–6 feet tall in late summer and fall above bold, compound foliage, but is distinguished by its leaf shape — the leaflets are broader and more coarsely toothed, resembling those of a raspberry, hence the species name rubifolia. It is a magnificent specimen plant for large, shaded native gardens; purchase only nursery-propagated stock and never collect from the wild, as natural populations are small and threatened. Long-lived and deer-resistant once established.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Appalachian mountain mint is a vigorous, spreading perennial with intensely minty foliage and exceptional pollinator value.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Appalachian mountain mint is a spreading native perennial of the eastern US with narrowly linear, intensely minty-scented leaves and flat-topped clusters of tiny white flowers with purple spots from July through September that are exceptional pollinator magnets — research has found that mountain mint species attract more species of native bees per plant than virtually any other wildflower, making this one of the highest-value pollinator plants for the eastern garden. It spreads by rhizomes to form colonies but is not aggressively invasive and can be controlled by edging; cut plants to the ground in fall. The crushed foliage repels insects and can be used as a natural mint flavoring. A workhorse plant for native meadows, rain gardens, and sunny to lightly shaded borders.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in dry to moist, well-drained, strongly acidic, sandy or rocky soil in partial shade to full sun; eastern turkeybeard is specific to pine barrens and mountain slopes in strongly acidic soils and resents fertile or alkaline conditions.

directions: Plant in dry to moist, well-drained, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy or rocky soil in partial shade to full sun; eastern turkeybeard grows naturally in pine barrens, sandhills, and rocky mountain slopes from New Jersey to Georgia in strictly acidic, nutrient-poor, fire-adapted communities that are difficult to replicate in gardens but rewarding to attempt. Xerophyllum asphodeloides produces a dense clump of stiff, needle-like, grass-like leaves up to 24 inches long from which arises in late spring a tall, stout flowering stem bearing an impressive, dense, cylindrical spike of tiny, fragrant, creamy white flowers 6–12 inches long that are spectacular in bloom and an important pollen source for native bees. Individual plants may take several years to bloom but are very long-lived; the clump expands slowly. Do not add compost, lime, or fertilizer — this plant thrives on infertility and acidity. An outstanding accent plant for acid-soil gardens and pine barren restoration.
sources: New Jersey Pine Barrens Native Plant Society

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, strongly acidic, sandy or peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; white colicroot grows naturally in acidic, seasonally wet savannas, flatwoods, and bogs and requires specific acidic, low-nutrient soil conditions — do not add lime or fertilizer.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy or peaty, low-nutrient soil in partial shade to full sun; white colicroot is a native perennial of the eastern and southeastern US coastal plain and Piedmont, growing naturally in open, wet, acidic savannas, pine flatwoods, bogs, and roadside ditches, where it produces a flat basal rosette of smooth, pale-green, lance-shaped leaves and a tall, wand-like spike of small, mealy-white, tubular flowers with a distinctive bumpy, granular texture — the species name farinosa means 'floury' and refers to the rough, mealy surface of the flower tube. In bloom from May to August, the white wand-like spikes are one of the more distinctive and unusual native wildflower forms and are excellent for naturalistic planting with pitcher plants, sundews, and other bog-community species. The plant has a long history of use in traditional Appalachian and Native American medicine for digestive ailments. Never add lime, compost, or fertilizer to the planting site.
